text: The surgeon spaces the robotic ports approximately six to seven centimeters apart, adjusting the left lower quadrant port to prevent thigh collision. The operation begins with the reduction of incarcerated omental contents, assisted by the bedside assistant. Due to the absence of infraumbilical posterior rectus sheath and peritoneum, the TAP operation is deemed unsuitable. The falciform fat is excised to improve mesh apposition to the fascia, and the ligamentum teres is divided upon reaching the correct anatomical plane.

The surgeon uses a six-inch suture to close the small hernia, either vertically or transversely. If the umbilical stock is not tethered, the bedside assistant pushes down on the umbilical skin while the surgeon carefully takes a small bite of the hernia sac to invert the umbilicus.

The surgeon lays sutures without tightening them to facilitate grabbing the umbilical stock through a small defect. A Bard Ventralight ST mesh is used, with a pre-placed V-Loc 180 suture in the center to suspend the mesh. The suture is pushed through the mesh onto the coated side, ensuring at least a four-centimeter overlap for smaller defects. The mesh is secured by suturing towards the surgeon.

The surgeon uses a longer 12-inch V-lock suture to perform a baseball stitch circumferentially around the mesh, moving counterclockwise. As the suture approaches the area of the diastasis, the surgeon adjusts the stitch placement inward on the mesh to ensure coverage in case of suture pull-through. The falciform fat will later be used to cover the unsecured area of the mesh.

The surgeon continues suturing around the periphery of the mesh after passing the diastasis, securing the central part of the mesh to the abdominal wall to minimize the pocket and reduce seromas. The video is sped up to show the straightforward suturing process, alternating between flesh-to-mesh and mesh-to-flesh techniques.

The surgeon secures the falciform fat to the abdominal wall using absorbable braided sutures to cover the portion of the mesh not secured at the edge. The mesh is clearly visible, particularly the ipsilateral edge, aided by effective port planning.
